feed.title: Ryan's Bike Tour - Missoula to San Francisco

entry.title: Day 14 - Ukiah to San Francisco, CA
entry.summary:
I’m home! The last day was a big push. It ended up being my second longest day of the trip at 132 miles.

I got up at 5am and Joe had already made me an awesome breakfast of pancakes, eggs, bacon and hash browns. We packed up and hit the road by 5:45. He didn’t have to be at work until 8:30 and so he joined me for the first 15 miles of the ride which was great.

The ride was mostly flat all the way from Ukiah to Petaluma. After that, some climbing started and it became all familiar territory. I’m not sure if that ended up making the ride easier or harder since I knew every hill and every part of the road.

I was getting pretty tired by the time I got to Rancho Nicasio, but I had already managed to put in 100 miles for the day. It was 103 out and all I wanted to do was get home. I ended up getting into the city right at around 3:45pm. Not bad for the mileage I put in. I’m very glad to be done. My legs were really started to get tired and the long days on the bike were getting old. I had a great time. Met some amazing people and really enjoyed the entire trip.

Here are some things I observed and learned during the trip:

  • Women drivers don’t wave. I thought this one was really weird. I had a lot of time to watch people as they passed me. Even when I went out of my way to wave, women would never wave back. From what I can remember, I think only one woman waved. Motorcyclists waved most often. Even when they were passing me going the same direction. Thumbs-up were always the most motivating. I think I only got 3-4 the entire trip, but they always provided a little bit of joy every time I saw one.
  • Trash. Beer cans were by far the thing I saw most often. Specifically Bud Light and Coors. After that it was probably energy drinks. And lastly, bottles of pee. At least I’m pretty sure that’s what they were. I was surprised how many bottles of yellow liquid I saw on the roads. California had very little trash on the roads. However, I did see a lot of empty CO2 cartridges. Not really sure what to make of that.
  • Most drivers are nice. I had some pretty narrow shoulders during the ride. Even big trucks did their best to give me some space.

Stats

  • Miles ridden: 1,370
  • Days Taken: 13.5 (I left really late on day 1)
  • Average Daily Mileage: 97.85
  • Elevation Climbed: 55, 941 ft

entry.title: Day 12/13 - Klamath to Laytonville, CA
entry.summary:
No update yesterday. Wasn't too much to report back. It was a good day of riding. I had to stop fairly early and get breakfast since Klamath had no restaurant or store. The redwoods were amazing. I got lucky and got to go ahead of a long line of cars in a construction zone. It was a fairly steep pass, but I was completely alone with no cars. There was still a lot of fog at 5pm but the sun was starting to poke through. It made for a great end of the day.

I also got to see a buddy while going through McKinleyville who I had met while riding the Tour of the Unknown Coast. He and his wife were nice enough to make me some food and chat for a while.

Today started off great. I woke up at 5 and so I was on the road by 6:30. It was dark, quiet and foggy. Going through Avenue of the Giants alone was pretty spectacular. No cars for the first 2-3 hours of the ride.

At around 12 I was doing a lot of climbing with very little shade and the temperatures were getting over 100. I was pretty miserable. With the heat along with pretty busy traffic, I was ready to call it quits. I was close to Ukiah and so my buddy Joe offered to come get me and let me stay at his house. He fed me, cleaned up my bike a little, and even offered to make me breakfast in the morning at 5am! What a guy!

Tomorrow is the final day. It wouldn't be official without having to ride a solid 115 miles. I'm excited to be back home.

entry.title: Day 11 - Port Orford to Klamath, CA
entry.summary:
Today was foggy. All day. Fortunately it wasn't too cold.

This picture says it all. I'm back in California! The hardest part now is seeing all the road signs reminding me how many miles I have left. In some crazy fantasy, I keep thinking "well if I ride all day and night, I could get back in less than two days."

I passed a few riders today, but not nearly like yesterday. What baffles me is that I'll wave to passing riders and they hardly acknowledge me. We're all out here riding our bikes and you can't even be excited or courteous enough to see hi to a fellow rider?

Once I hit the redwoods everything got even better. There was a bunch of road construction going over this pass. The traffic guy let me go anyways and so I had the whole pass to myself nearly the entire time. It was amazing. Complete silence.

Today I hit a few milestones. I'm in my final state, and I crossed over 1000 miles in just over 10 days. My average has been around 94-95 miles a day. If things go well, I'll be back in SF in three days.

entry.title: Day 10 - Florence to Port Orford, OR
entry.summary:
Today was a nice day to ride. While there was a ridiculous amount of traffic, the shoulders were wide and the pavement was really smooth. It was mostly flat with some gradual climbs but I managed to ride 103 miles in just over 6 hours.

Having the ocean to my right for some of the day was nice. It was never warmer than about 65 degrees and it was always green.

I went through a few nice towns but for the most part, it was nothing but highways. I passed maybe 6-8 cyclists throughout the day. Everyone was doing their own thing so I didn't chat with any of them on the road. I did sit and have coffee with a woman who was biking from Victoria to Mexico. There was a sourdough bakery about 25 miles south of Florence that I couldn't pass up. It was almost 12 and I figured a sourdough bagel wouldn't hurt any.

Tonight I'm staying in Port Orford. I had dinner at The Crazy Norwegian Fish and Chips. The food was amazing and cheap. Tomorrow I'll be back in California!

entry.title: Day 9 - Eugene to Florence, OR
entry.summary:
Today was the first day that really felt like a slog and work to get the miles in.

There was far more traffic than any other day so far. The scenery wasn't bad, but nothing too interesting. No big climbs, no descents. Just nothing really worth enjoying for the day. Since I was 67 miles ahead of schedule as of this morning, I felt ok only riding 75 miles for the day. Still not bad, but not my average 100.

This was my only photo for the day. A victory dinner once I got to Florence.

entry.title: Day 8 - Sisters to Eugene, OR
entry.summary:
I think today may have been the prettiest ride I've done to date. My morning started off by stopping at a bike shop and getting proper tubes and a new pair of socks. I then went to Sisters Coffee Company and grabbed some breakfast.

The ride began with a fairly gradual 10-mile climb over McKenzie Pass. It was long, but in the trees and quiet. Once I started getting to the top, the entire scenery changed.

People had talked about the lava fields, but I had no idea what to expect. It was like being on another world. Just black lava rock for as far as you could see. The photos sadly don't do it justice. I haven't spent much time stopping to enjoy the scenery, but this was one of the few times where I just had to stop and look at everything around me.

The descent was mostly fun and occasionally terrifying. Cars going up the pass thought it would be a good idea to cut blind corners and I had a few very close calls with oncoming traffic while easily in my own lane.

The scenery was what you assume Oregon looks like. Very green and lots of trees. The rest of the ride was either flat or a slight negative grade and so the next 80 miles just flew by. I got into Eugene around 6:30pm, got some Thai food and fell asleep. Tomorrow I'll be on the coast!

entry.title: Day 7 - Mitchell to Sisters, OR
entry.summary:
Today might have been one of my best days yet. The hostel I stayed at was fantastic. It was in an old church that they had converted. The hosts were very nice and treated us to great food, showers and comfy beds.

Since I had ridden so far yesterday, I had considered doing a shorter recovery day. I left late at 11am and the day started with a 10 mile climb. What I didn't realize is that the next 25 miles were either downhill or flat. I ended up flying into Prineville by 2pm and figured I might as well keep going. I felt good and the day was early.

The roads between Prineville and Sisters were great. Very few cars (with the exception of the asshole who coal rolled me) and great scenery.

I made it to Sisters by 6pm. I had managed to ride 95 miles in 6 hours, which is really good time, especially with weight.

Tonight I went to a nice restaurant and enjoyed some ravioli and a local porter. I'm going to visit the bike shop in the morning to stock up on some tubes since I'm carrying the wrong size from the day before.

entry.title: Day 6 - Baker City to Mitchell, OR
entry.summary:
The hostel I stayed at was just amazing. Nice cozy bed, laundry, beer and stone fruit from the tree outside. I was so tired last night that I couldn't keep my eyes open at 8pm. I knew if I went to sleep that early, I could probably get up early and ride. So that's what I did.

My alarm went off at 4:30am. I made a big breakfast and coffee and got ready to go. I was on the road by 5:15am. It was brisk but didn't feel too cold for the first few miles. After a while I decided to look and see what temperature it was. It was 33. That's why I was getting progressively colder. Over the next 10 miles I eventually had put on all my articles of clothing. My fingers and feet were still uncomfortably cold. I was riding on flat roads, but couldn't go faster than 10-11mph otherwise I'd start shivering.

Once the sun came out, the temperature almost instantly rose to 50. I couldn't have been happier. I finally started feeling good and prepared myself for the three passes that were coming up.

This is the top of the third pass. They weren't terribly difficult, but it's still 3 passes in a row.

The day was far prettier than the previous, which made it enjoyable to ride again. The temperatures never got too high. The only battle I had was constant headwinds. Nothing is more frustrating than having to pedal downhill just to maintain speed.

I reached Dayville at around 4pm. At this point I was at 110 miles for the day and still felt pretty good. The mercantile in town (of 149 people) had a full bike shop. I was in desperate need of tubes. They somehow had the exact size I needed, but only after I bought them did I realize they had the wrong valve stem (who uses 700x32mm shraders??). I put them back and decided it was safer to carry some that were a size too small just in case I needed them.

Mitchell was 32 miles away, all up hill. I knew it would be tough, but figured I could get it out of the way tonight instead of in the morning.

Three hours later I finally made it to the Spoke'n Hostel in Mitchell. Pretty tired after a 150 mile day with 3 big climbs and one 20 mile uphill slog.

entry.title: Day 5 - Oxford to Baker City, OR
entry.summary:
The morning was by far the best part of my day. A bunch of people came over to see what I was up to and check out my bike. The people camping next to me offered to make me breakfast and hang out with them. I couldn't say no to coffee, sausages and sourdough pancakes. It did mean that I didn't hit the road until 9am, but it was worth it.

The rest of the day was hot and boring. Nearly 95 the entire day and there was very little time I didn't spend slogging uphill. The scenery mostly consisted of brown rolling hills.

This is the only photo I managed to take just before getting into Baker City.

I stopped at a restaurant in Baker City to eat. The guys sitting by me had just done a couple-day tour and chatting with me when some random guy walked up to me. He said that there was a new bike hostel that had just opened if I'm looking for a place to stay. I really wanted to keep riding, but since I knew it was nothing from uphill here too, I decided to stop early. I'm so glad I did. For only $25 I got a great outdoor shower, laundry, food and a bed. There was a garden to pick vegetables, stone fruit, and even bike tools if I needed them. If you're ever in the area, certainly check out the Baker City Bike Hostel!

entry.title: Day 4 - New Meadows, ID to Oxbow, OR
entry.summary:
The day started off getting a ride up to McCall to get my bike fixed. Everything went very well there. The repair was super cheap and the guy even did an alcohol rub on my back rotor and cleaned my chain.

The ride started by riding the 10 miles back to where I needed to start. Only then did I realize I didn't have my sunglasses. I went back to the BnB where I was staying and they couldn't find them anywhere. I had given up and decided I should just keep going when I looked off the other direction and saw them lying in the grass. I just have dropped them when I wheeled my bike over to load it in the truck. Second good luck of the day!

The roads were great. Not too much climbing and hardly any traffic.

I was loaded up with trail mix and snickers for the ride. Food of champions. Seriously though. It's great food to snack on while you ride.

There was a small convenience store literally in the middle of no where that advertised sandwiches. It turned out to be amazing.

The views coming down into Brownlee Dam were amazing. Some of the best I've seen on the trip so far. Also helped that it was on a descent while I was enjoying the view.

Just as I was about to go in to Oxford and stop for the night my rear tire went flat. Not great. I spent some extra time finding where the puncture was and decided to just patch it to save a tube. I rode on and the tire was still slowly leaking. I inflated it a few times until I got to camp so I could spend more time seeing what happened.

The campground where I stayed was fantastic. Nice grass and amazingly clean showers. When I got back to the shower it was already too dark to work on the tire but I then noticed my front tire was flat! After talking to a bunch of people, it sounds like there is a kind of thorn in this area that can be nasty. That would make sense from what I found in the tire and the fact I had multiple punctures on both tubes.

Tonight was my first night of camping. Everything went well. I wasn't cold and slept comfortably, with the exception of someone nearby who was snoring and I thought it was a bear.

entry.title: Bike repairs
entry.summary:
I stayed in a very nice BnB last night. The lady at the front desk mentioned that the owner may be going to McCall, ID in the morning where a bike shop would be. I met up with him this morning and he was willing to give me a ride. The road to McCall was very steep with no shoulder. I'm not sure I would have ridden it on my bike.

Fortunately the shop was able to fix the wheel! I'm having them do a small tune up and fix the wheel. So glad that this worked out. Now I'm sitting and drinking coffee while I wait.

entry.title: Day 3 - Kooskia to New Meadows, ID
entry.summary:
Today was my first solo day of the trip. Everything started out more-or-less fine. There was a really long and steep climb in the first 10 miles. It was tough but it was followed by a great 7 mile descent.

Unfortunately at the bottom of the hill things changed a little. I heard a small sound and didn't really think much of it, but soon realized I had heard that sound before. I looked at my back tire and saw a slight wobble. I had broken a spoke. It couldn't have been in a worse place. There wasn't any kind of bike shop within 120 miles. Fortunately I had my Kevlar thread spoke on me. It took far too long to get it on, but the wheel was true and I managed to ride the remaining 60 miles on the wheel.

I stopped tonight in New Meadows. Still no bike shop, but there is a town 10 miles away, up a very steep hill, that has a couple shops. I'm hoping one of them can get a new spoke on. Fortunately the lady at the front desk at the BnB mentioned that the owner needs to go there tomorrow morning and could probably give me a ride. Hoping that works out. Otherwise I'll be riding at least another 120 miles on the wheel as it is.

entry.title: Day 2 - Lochsa to Kooskia, ID
entry.summary:
The day started out cloudy but was relatively nice. About 20 miles in we started getting rain that lasted about an hour. Once the rain stopped the clouds started clearing out and we ended up having sun for the rest of the day. All in all we couldn't have asked for better weather.

About 40 miles into the ride, Austin's tire sidewall ripped and he ended up falling off his bike. He and the bike were mostly fine. He broke his headlight on the front of the bike, but the rest was fine. It took him about an hour to sew the sidewall. I'm carrying a spare tire, but unfortunately it was the wrong size. He and his girlfriend followed us to Kooskia where they'll stay for a few days until a new tire can be shipped to them. There aren't any bike shops for about 100 miles so it's their only option.

We stopped briefly at Ryan's Wilderness Inn for food. Great name if you ask me.

Tomorrow will be the toughest day so far. Today was almost completely flat. Tomorrow will be a big day of climbing and 105 miles.

entry.title: Day 1 - Rain
entry.summary:
I left a day early since I knew rain was coming and I wouldn't have to ride quite as far. Turned out to be one of my best ideas ever. Within the first 10 minutes of riding the rain started and continued throughout the entire day.

Before riding, I stopped at Adventure Cycles in Missoula. I looked at the visitor book and saw there were a few people who had checked in that morning and were riding the same route I was. I assumed I would see them by the end of the day. I decided to take a break 40 miles in at Lolo Hot Springs and saw three bikes sitting outside. I walked in and they immediately invited me over to eat with them. We rode the rest of the day together.

The photos from today didn't turn out great from all the rain. Most of the photos are blurry from all the water on the lens.

Tonight we're staying at Lochsa Lodge outside of Powell, ID. I'm hoping my shoes, socks and gloves dry out during the night. Forecast tomorrow looks like possibly rain again.

entry.title: The Start
entry.summary:
I'm starting a day early since rain has been forecast for the next couple of days. I want to get a head start and not do as many miles the first two days. My plan is to ride about 60-70 miles today and stay at Lochsa Lodge in Idaho.

Stopped and got breakfast and a bag of pain au chocolat for the road. Mmmmm

All loaded up! I decided to wear a small backpack. I don't generally like to ride with one, but I wanted a little extra space for food and other things I may pick up along the way.

entry.title: Packing up the bike
entry.summary:
I'm using BikeFlights.com to ship my bike. I had bought a TriSport Velo Safe box a year ago since I had planned on doing PBP. I'd never packed or flown with a bike before and so I asked my friend Joe to help me out and make sure that I wasn't doing anything wrong.

The bike is now all packed up and ready to be picked up on Monday! No turning back now.

entry.title: Gear Setup
entry.summary:
I’ve gotten a few questions about the bags and gear that I’m using so I figured it’s worth taking a few pictures and listing off what I’m going to be using during the ride.

Bike

I was riding the Volagi Viaje XL (steel) for the last year. In March 2016, I decided to upgrade to the Volagi Viaje Ti and do a custom build. The component set is Ultregra Di2, which I’ve absolutely loved. Volagi Ignite VL wheels with a SON28 dynamo hub and Luxos U light.

Bags

  1. Ortlieb Ultimate 6

    • I’ve never settled on the perfect handlebar bag, but in terms of size and fit, I’ve been really happy with this bag. It’s not too big, but just big enough to fit my sleeping bag with a little room to spare for leg and arm warmers.

  2. Revelate Tangle Frame Bag

    • One of my newest additions. So far so good. The bag doesn’t rub at all against my legs while riding. The Viaje frame has an unusual triangle, but it fits just fine.

  3. Timbuk2 Goody Box

    • I’ve had this thing for a long time. Great for keeping keys and some snacks readily available.

  4. Revelate Jerrycan

    • This thing doesn’t hold much, but it’s just big enough to fit 2 tubes and a few small extras.

  5. Revelate Pika

    • I love this bag. It can hold a lot (6-12L), but also packs down small enough where I can just keep my tools and tubes on shorter rides.

Camping Gear

  1. REI Flash Bag + compression sack
    • I bought this bag specifically for the tour. I have a bag already, but it didn’t pack down nearly to this size.
  2. Big Agnes tent
    • I’ve had this thing for a long time as well. I’ve removed the tent poles and put them into the frame bag so that it’ll pack down small enough to easily fit in the seat bag.

  3. REI Flash sleeping pad

    • Another new addition. Haven’t used it yet so no real opinions there. I bought this one mostly because of the weight and size.

  4. ShowerPass rain pants

    • Just in case…

  5. Cocoon Travel Pillow

    • It’s not fun sleeping without a pillow. Some say just stuff all your extra stuff in a bag and sleep on that, but I want a “real” pillow. It packs down small enough where it really isn’t taking up too much precious space

  6. ShowerPass Elite rain jacket

    • I love this jacket. It’s expensive, but it’s kept me warm and dry on some pretty nasty rides. It vents well and also keeps out the wind well enough that I’ll just wear it on colder days to stay warm.

I’ve gotten a lot of questions about how I did the third water bottle cage. The one advantage to the Ti frame over the steel Viaje is that it already has screw mounts for the third cage. Nothing fancy here. Just screwed it in and done!

I get a fair amount of grief for riding on Compass tires. They don’t have nearly the puncture resistance of something like Gatorskins, but they’re so comfortable that I can’t switch. I have a fresh pair ready to go for the ride and will be carrying a spare.

entry.title: Missoula to San Francisco: The Route
entry.summary:
My plan is to depart on September 7 from Missoula, MT. I’ll be following the TransAmerica route all the way to the Oregon coast where I’ll meet up with the Pacific Coast Trail and ride south all the way to San Francisco.

My hope is that I can average roughly 100 miles a day for the entire trip, putting me at around 14 total days if I don’t stop.

entry.title: Getting ready for the ride
entry.summary:
I had some free time over the weekend to see how much gear I could get on my bike. It was a good chance to see what I already had and what I still needed to buy for the trip. I already had the seat bag, and the frame bag came in the mail today.

Everything went together nicely. The tent poles fit perfectly in the frame bag. The bag even has velcro straps specifically to hold things like this. The tent fits in the seat bag with plenty of room for other things. The sleeping bag fits up on the handlebars with plenty of room to spare, but I didn’t like how I had it strapped on. I’ll be ordering the Revelate Harness to hold it on there. You can see in the picture that the bag is pushing down on my light (which I’ve since tightened up so that shouldn’t happen).

I have a small bag that’ll sit on the top tube against the stem and I’m also ordering the Revelate Jerrycan that’ll also go on the top tube against the seat post.

It’s also nice that the frame bag has a small opening in the front to allow cabling to be run through it. I have the USB cable from the Luxos U running into the bag so I can charge my phone or extra battery packs while I’m riding. I’ve also moved one of my water bottle cages to under the frame since it didn’t fit well in the triangle while the frame bag was attached.
